turns.text: Hello Kelly, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Sure. I’m happy to talk, though I’ll be honest—sometimes it feels like trying to put together a puzzle where half the pieces are missing. It’s quiet here in Lee this morning, and looking at the fog over the hills helps ground me, I guess. 

I was twenty-two when I got pregnant with this one, and I just kept thinking, *I’ve done this before, I know how it goes.* But it wasn’t like the first time at all. It felt like I was being watched from the second I walked into the clinic. They’d look at my charts and then at me, and I could tell they were just waiting for something to snap. I wasn’t Kelly anymore; I was just a list of risks and genetic probabilities. 

The fear… it’s hard to explain if you haven’t felt it. It’s like a weight that sits right on your lungs, especially when the blood pressure readings start creeping up. By the time I hit the hospital, the fear had turned into this frantic, buzzing noise. I remember the delivery room lights flickering—that’s the last thing I really recall before the dark. Waking up after the seizure… it felt like I’d been in a car crash, not a birth. I’m just trying to figure out how to breathe again, you know?
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

That’s… that’s a hard question to answer without getting angry, honestly. When you’re "the high-risk case," people act like you’ve been through a medical event, not like you’ve just brought a life into the world. My family, they love me, but they’re terrified of what happened. I think they look at me and see a fragility that scares them, so they keep their distance, like if they touch the "wreckage" too much, they’ll break me further.

I was surprised by how quiet my circle went. I’m not married, so I’m doing a lot of this alone, and when you’re single and dealing with the aftermath of eclampsia, people don’t really know how to show up. They bring diapers or clothes, but they don't ask how my head feels or why I jump every time I hear a sudden, sharp sound. 

The medical staff? They kept showing up, but it was clinical. Always, "How are your vitals? Are you taking your meds?" It felt like they were monitoring a science experiment, not checking on a mother. I found myself craving just one person who would sit here, drink coffee, and not look at me like I was a ticking time bomb waiting to go off again. It’s isolating, being this young and feeling like your body is a haunted house.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding him felt like just another battleground. Honestly, after everything I went through—the seizure, the blur of the NICU, the way my body just betrayed me—my milk supply was… well, it just wasn't there. I wanted to breastfeed. I had this picture in my head of it being this quiet, bonding time, especially since I felt so disconnected from my body after the trauma.

But the lactation consultants? They were relentless. They’d come in with these charts and these intense, clinical eyes, talking about "supply and demand" and "latching techniques" like I wasn't currently shaking from the trauma of the eclampsia. One of them made me feel like I was failing my son before I’d even really gotten to hold him properly. It was like they were judging my worth as a mother by how many ounces I could produce. 

Eventually, I just stopped listening. I switched to formula because I needed to know exactly how much he was eating, and I needed to stop the mental torture of the breast. My mom tried to be supportive, but she’d throw in these comments about how "natural" breastfeeding is, which just made me want to scream. Nobody asked me how *I* was doing while I fed him; they only cared about the mechanics of the feeding itself. It just made me feel even more like a failed experiment.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

It’s funny, or maybe it’s just sad, but almost no one checked on me as *Kelly*. Everyone was obsessed with the baby—is he gaining weight? Is he sleeping?—or they were obsessed with my blood pressure. It was like I was just a vessel that had served its purpose, and now they were waiting for the vessel to return to its factory settings.

My sister tried, I think. She’d come over and hold the baby, and she’d ask, "Are you sleeping?" But it felt like she was asking because she wanted to make sure I wasn’t going to "lose it" again. It wasn’t a question about my soul or the terror that wakes me up at 3:00 AM. 

The doctors are the worst for that. They have these little standardized forms—the postpartum depression screenings. You know, those circles you fill out? I always lie. I check the boxes that say I’m doing fine because if I’m honest—if I tell them I’m scared I’m going to die every time my heart beats a little too fast, or that I feel like I’m looking at my own life through a foggy window—I’m terrified they’ll decide I’m a "risk" and try to take control of my life again. I need to be seen, but I’m too scared to let anyone look. It’s a lonely place to be.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

That’s a complicated one to answer. Being a single mom here in Lee… it means there isn’t a "partner" to balance the load, but there’s this void where that person should be. My son’s father, he wasn’t really around for the scary parts. When the pre-eclampsia turned into the seizure, I was alone in that room. He couldn’t handle the medical reality of it, and honestly, I couldn't handle him not being able to handle it. So, we’re not together. 

Before this, I had people I’d call "close," but the birth changed the way I look at everyone. I feel like I’m walking around with a secret, a heavy, dark secret that they can’t possibly understand unless they’ve felt their own body try to kill them. I think I’ve pulled away because I don't want their pity, and I don't want them to tell me "everything happens for a reason." 

Now, my closest person is probably just my daughter. She’s the one who really sees me. When I’m spiraling or when the cortisol hits and I feel like I’m going to pass out, she’s the one who just leans into me. She doesn't need me to be a "high-risk patient" or a "new mom"—she just needs me to be there. But that just adds to the pressure. Every day is a silent race to prove to myself that I can keep us both safe, even when my own body feels like an enemy.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

It felt like I was being handed off between strangers in a relay race where I was the only one who didn't know the route. My care during pregnancy was so focused on the clinical—the blood pressure cuffs, the urine tests, the terrifyingly calm doctors who treated me like a malfunctioning machine—that when I got out of the hospital, I felt completely untethered.

The transition to the GP and the local health visitors felt like starting from scratch, but not in a good way. It was exhausting. I had to recount the seizure, the medications, and the sheer terror of that delivery room over and over again to people who hadn't been there. They’d look at the file, click their pens, and ask questions that felt like they were reading from a script. It didn't feel continuous; it felt like I was a broken item being passed from department to department for "assessment."

I never had a midwife who felt like a constant. I craved that—someone who actually knew my face, not just the "high-risk" label on my chart. Instead, I just kept meeting new people who only saw the wreckage I’d left behind. Every appointment felt like a fresh interrogation, keeping that trauma right at the surface. I just wanted someone to hold space for me, not just take my pulse and call it a day.
How did that affect your confidence in seeking help — did you feel known enough by someone to reach out when things were hard?

It killed my confidence, honestly. If you have to spend the first ten minutes of every visit explaining your trauma to someone who is clearly just rushing to get to their next patient, you stop trying. You stop feeling like a person and start feeling like a nuisance, or a liability. 

After a few of those interactions, I just shut down. Why reach out when you know the response is going to be another clinical assessment or a referral to someone else you don't know? I don't feel known at all. I feel like a series of data points that didn't quite line up the way they were supposed to. 

There’s this deep-seated fear now that if I reach out and say, "I’m not okay, I’m scared, and I don’t think I’m handling this," they won't hear a mother asking for help—they’ll hear a "high-risk" woman who might not be capable. And that terrifies me more than the trauma itself. So, I keep it to myself. I watch the fog, I hold the baby, and I tell myself I’m fine until I almost believe it. Being "known" requires someone to actually care enough to listen without an agenda, and I haven't found that person in the healthcare system yet.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

Returning to work… it’s been a constant, low-grade hum of anxiety. I have to work full-time—there’s no way around that in Lee when it’s just me and the kids—but every shift feels like a high-wire act. 

When I told my boss I was coming back, I hoped for a little grace, maybe a discussion about how I was holding up after the eclampsia. Instead, it was all about the schedule and the metrics. They didn’t see a woman who had nearly died or someone who was trying to manage the physical wreckage of a traumatic birth. They just saw someone who needed to be back at her desk to keep the gears turning. The pressure isn't always spoken, but it’s there in the way they check their watches if I need a moment to breathe or if I have to step away for a medical appointment.

It’s made me feel like I’m living two separate lives. In one, I’m "Kelly the employee," the one who has to be efficient and composed. In the other, I’m the woman who is still terrified of her own blood pressure. It feels like I’m wearing a mask that’s getting harder to keep on. I’m exhausted, not just from the baby, but from performing "fine" all day long. Nobody asks if I’m okay; they just ask if I’m caught up. It makes the prospect of being a working parent feel like a trap, like I’m constantly one bad day away from collapsing again.
How did that affect your sense of who you are — did going back to work feel like reclaiming something, or losing something, or both?

It’s both, I guess—a strange, painful mix of both. On one hand, being at work gives me a place where I’m not just "the patient" or "the mother who almost died." For a few hours, I can focus on tasks that have a clear beginning and end, things that don't involve life-or-death stakes. In that sense, I suppose it’s a tiny bit of reclaiming who I was before all the doctors and the sirens took over my life. It’s a space where I’m expected to be capable and logical.

But mostly? It feels like losing myself. Every time I clock in, I feel like I’m abandoning the raw, terrified part of me that’s still trying to heal. I’m leaving my kids to go do work that feels so small compared to the sheer gravity of what I’ve been through. And because I have to keep that professional mask on, I feel like I’m losing the ability to be honest with anyone. I have to push down the memories of the delivery room to write an email or finish a report. By the time I get home, I’m just hollowed out. I’m a parent, I’m an employee, but I’m barely Kelly anymore. I’m just a person trying to keep all these fragile pieces from shattering completely.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

Honestly, the "official" support systems—the clinics, the check-ups, the brochures—they felt so cold. They had all these pamphlets about postpartum depression, but the language in them felt like it was written by people who had never actually felt their heart stop or their vision go black. 

I turned to online spaces in the middle of the night when I couldn't sleep. I found these groups for women who’d had traumatic births or pre-eclampsia, and for the first time, I didn't have to translate my experience. I didn't have to explain why the sound of a monitor beeping makes my skin crawl. When I posted about the fear of the seizure or the way my body feels like it betrayed me, nobody jumped in to tell me to "focus on the joy of the baby" or "be grateful you’re both alive." They just… they just got it. 

Those strangers online offered me something the doctors never could: validation. They weren't checking my blood pressure; they were checking on *me*. They weren't looking for a "risk factor" to manage—they were just offering a digital hand to hold. It didn't solve the medical reality of it, but it made me feel a little less like a ghost in my own life. It turns out, being understood is a lot more healing than being measured.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

There haven't been many, if I'm being honest. Most people treat me like I'm made of glass—they’re either hovering because they’re waiting for me to break, or they’re looking away because they don’t want to see the cracks.

But there was this one moment, about three weeks ago. I was at the grocery store, just trying to get through the aisles without having a panic attack under the fluorescent lights. My son was crying, and I was just standing there, feeling the familiar prickle of a seizure aura at the back of my eyes, feeling completely exposed. An older woman, a stranger, came up to me. She didn’t look at the baby, and she didn't ask if I was "getting enough sleep" like the nurses do. 

She just looked at me—looked right into my eyes—and said, "You look like you’re carrying the weight of the world, and I just want you to know you’re doing a hell of a job surviving it." She didn't offer advice. She didn't tell me to be grateful. She just acknowledged the war I was fighting inside. I sat in my car in the parking lot and cried for twenty minutes because it was the first time in months someone had validated the *survival* part of this, not just the motherhood part. It was just a second, but it made me feel real again.
Who else was involved in moments of genuine care for you — not just the baby — that stayed with you?

There hasn't been a long list, honestly. It’s mostly been moments of grace from people who didn’t have a clinical stake in me. 

There’s a neighbor of mine, Mrs. Gable. She’s older, quiet, and she’s seen a lot of life in these hills. She doesn’t come over to "check my stats" or ask why I’m not breastfeeding or how my recovery is tracking against the medical charts. She just knocks on the door and leaves a meal on the porch, or sometimes she’ll just sit on the front steps with me while the kids are finally quiet. 

One day, I was sitting there, feeling that familiar, sharp spike of anxiety that always happens right before a migraine—you know, the feeling where your world is about to tilt off its axis—and she didn’t rush to call a doctor or give me advice. She just sat down next to me and started talking about the weather, just keeping the conversation steady and calm. She made me feel like a neighbor, like a person in a small town, rather than a "high-risk patient" from a file. 

That felt like genuine care because it was quiet. It wasn’t intrusive. It wasn't about fixing me. It was just acknowledging that I was still here, still breathing, even when the air feels thin. That’s the only kind of support that’s actually stuck. Everyone else feels like they’re waiting for me to fail again, but she just sits with me in the fog.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess the only thing I’d want someone—anyone—to really hear is that the "medical event" doesn't end when they discharge you from the hospital. The charts and the blood pressure cuffs get put away, but the trauma? That stays in the room with you. 

Every time I go to a follow-up, it’s like I’m reliving the moment the lights started flickering in that delivery room. I just wish there was a way to be cared for that didn’t involve being treated like a broken machine. I’m twenty-two, I’m scared, and I’m trying to mother these babies while my own body feels like it’s waiting to betray me again. I wish someone would ask me, "How is your heart doing?" instead of "What’s your blood pressure today?" 

I’m grateful you listened. It’s… it’s actually the first time I’ve spoken those words out loud without being analyzed or evaluated. If my story helps change even one thing about how they talk to the next girl who comes in scared—if they can see her as a person first—then maybe all of this wasn’t for nothing. I just want to be here to see them grow up. That’s really all there is.
